original_abstract: The past two decades have witnessed the re-emergence of a system of international justice mechanisms, and the “institutionalisation” and “judicialisation” of international criminal justice, principally through the operation of formalised international and hybrid courts and tribunals. This followed a long period of impunity, during which there was virtually no legal accountability for the commission of atrocities and international crimes. After a slow beginning, several of these mechanisms then became active in the implementation of criminal justice against those accused of the most egregious crimes. All of a sudden, judges were required to adjudicate on complex and emotional factual and legal issues, largely without the benefit of significant practical experience. Over time, and particularly more recently, questions have been publically raised — even by judges themselves — as to the validity of several significant decisions. Highly visible controversies have emerged that have highlighted the difficulties in the judicial decision-making processes within these mechanisms of international justice. These challenge perceptions as to the “efficiency” of the justice being handed down. This article will discuss the applicable law used to determine and interpret legal rules in the international criminal arena, and then explore how these are increasingly challenged by both internal and external factors associated with the overarching context of the specific conflicts, and their political and social consequences.
humanized_abstract: International criminal justice: Is justice in jeopardy? The international criminal justice system, as we know it today, has only been rebuilt in the last two decades. The “institutionalisation” and “judicialisation” of international criminal justice have made international criminal courts and tribunals active and operational in seeking justice for victims of crimes in international law. The period of impunity lasting nearly thirty years was brought to an end with some tangible enforcement of international criminal law. The challenge before those international courts and tribunals has been enormous and most demanding on all sides. The new “genère” of international crimes’ judges had to adjudicate upon increasingly complex factual and legal issues concerning most serious crimes recognised by International Law, with few if any years of practical experience. This has exposed national and international justice to severe challenges. In some recent developments, in particular, in the context of ad hoc international criminal tribunals, the judicial reasoning supporting the majority of their decisions has been extensively scrutinised. In several occasions the decisions have even been qualified criticism and raised a number of questions at different levels. In particular a number of international criminal judges who have already sat in judgment in several occasions expressed, on occasion, concern over some of their landmark or significant findings. This has given rise to major and widely publicised disputes, raising questions and apprehension about whether justice is being delivered in such manner that victims of international crimes are properly served with justice according to applicable rules of international criminal law and having regard to the applicable process guarantees. The purpose of this paper is to reflect on the applicable law when deciding or interpreting provisions that make up the rules of International Criminal Law, and to look into potential conflicts with this applicable law when exercising criminal jurisdiction for the investigation and prosecution of international crimes, which has come to arise in practice over the last few years. It will further reflect on the impact of context on such exercise of criminal jurisdiction, having regard in particular to the politically and socially charged nature of criminal prosecution in international courts, such as ad hoc criminal tribunals for the former Yugoslavia and Rwanda or, increasingly, permanent criminal institutions such as the ICC, trying to highlight the nature of the challenges such Courts and tribunals encounter when they seek enforcement of crimes in international law in the post Cold War times.
